lens.included_activities: Independent US beet-sugar processors in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that repeatedly provide toll refining, contract processing, or a comparable outsourced manufacturing service to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Farmer-owned cooperative plants processing member-owner beets, branded or commodity sugar sales without an outsourced-service relationship, captive facilities, closed plants, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible revenue would arise from repeated fees or conversion margins for processing customer-owned or customer-directed beet inputs into refined sugar and by-products; ordinary sales of processor-owned sugar are not eligible service revenue.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader sugar and confectionery industry, not beet-only plants: production is 42.74% of employment, transportation/material moving 15.26%, maintenance 5.54%, office support 5.73%, and management 5.40%. I map administrative, planning, engineering-analysis, inspection, process-optimization, and selected control-room tasks to AI exposure while treating most physical production, material handling, maintenance, sanitation, and safety work as unexposed. Confectionery adds retail and hand-production roles absent from beet sugar, so direction of bias is uncertain; already extensive process control likely makes this estimate too high if not fully netted out.
a.rationale: Beet sugar is a continuous, capital-intensive process already run through centralized controls. Incremental AI can assist crop-intake forecasting, energy optimization, predictive maintenance, lab and quality analysis, operator decision support, scheduling, documentation, and back-office work, but it cannot substitute for most embodied plant operation during a compressed campaign.
a.change_evidence: Wage-weighted occupation rosters and task-time studies from at least four US beet factories, paired with inventories of existing DCS, laboratory automation, predictive maintenance, and administrative systems, would materially change a.
caveats: The occupational mix is four-digit NAICS and includes confectionery businesses unlike beet factories.
Current automation baselines are plant-specific and not publicly enumerated.
No eligible outsourced-service beet processor was identified, so operating evidence comes from the overall industry.

rho.bridge: Manufacturing-wide adoption surveys and an overseas beet-factory technology program measure AI uptake and technical feasibility, not five-year realized labor substitution in US LMM beet processors. The overseas program reports real-time NIR sensing and predictive models aimed at autopilot operation, while manufacturing surveys report 51% current AI use and 60% expected use by 2027. Large-company and vendor-selected evidence likely biases implementation upward; union work rules, legacy controls, cybersecurity, validation, and campaign downtime risk pull it down.
rho.rationale: Administrative copilots and decision support can be implemented within five years, and sensor-rich continuous plants are suitable for predictive control and maintenance. Yet a short annual slicing campaign makes failed integration expensive, and the sector's existing automation means remaining tasks are harder to remove.
rho.change_evidence: US beet-processor capital plans and post-implementation audits showing installed AI use cases, verified labor hours avoided, energy and recovery improvements, false alarms, cybersecurity reviews, and union impacts would materially move rho.
caveats: The beet-specific technology example is European and reports a development objective, not labor savings.
Survey use of AI may include pilots and lightweight tools without workforce impact.
Every US factory is unionized according to the industry association, adding implementation constraints.

e.rationale: The industry association states that all eight US beet-sugar manufacturers and all 20 factories are farmer-owned cooperative operations. Their core activity is processing owners' crops and marketing sugar, not supplying outsourced services to external customers. No qualifying independent toll or contract processor was found; the high bound allows classification or minority-service exceptions that public industry summaries may omit.
e.change_evidence: A legal-entity and revenue review of all eight manufacturers, including customer contracts, toll-processing revenue, ownership, and the four firms represented by the frozen LMM estimate, could confirm whether any external recurring service operation exists.
caveats: Cooperatives are firms, but processing member-owner crops is treated as captive to the owners rather than service to external customers.
The frozen n estimate of four may reflect revenue-sized cooperative entities or classification artifacts rather than eligible acquisition targets.
Public descriptions may not disclose small toll-processing arrangements.

s5.bridge: Broad US owner surveys indicate widespread age-driven succession, but the observed beet-sugar population is entirely cooperative and therefore lacks a conventional controlling owner whose retirement produces a sale. I heavily discount the broad five-year transfer signal to allow only rare demutualization, distressed asset sale, or sale of an independently controlled exception. The broad-owner proxy strongly biases the rate upward for cooperative beet processors.
s5.rationale: Member interests can turn over without a qualifying change of control, and cooperative boards can replace management without selling the enterprise. A plant closure or asset transfer is possible, but an eligible independent service firm first has to exist and then undergo a control sale.
s5.change_evidence: Cooperative bylaws, lender covenants, board strategic plans, demutualization provisions, and a 20-year history of US beet-factory ownership and asset transfers would establish the true qualifying-transfer frequency.
caveats: No eligible firm or qualifying control transfer was observed directly.
Management succession and changes in cooperative membership are excluded.
A population this small makes annualized probabilities unstable.

q.rationale: For a hypothetical toll processor, conversion fees would likely be negotiated against energy, recovery, uptime, and capital requirements, allowing some efficiency gains to remain with the plant until repricing. Specialized local assets, a narrow harvest radius, marketing allotments, and high switching costs support retention; transparent commodity economics, grower/customer bargaining, and union sharing limit it.
q.change_evidence: Actual tolling agreements, grower payment formulas, union contracts, sugar sales contracts, marketing-allotment records, and customer rebid histories would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No qualifying outsourced-service contract was found.
The estimate applies to a hypothetical eligible firm, not the observed cooperative population.
Federal sugar policy affects volumes and prices but does not directly reveal contractual sharing of AI savings.

d5.bridge: USDA forecasts total US sugar use at 12.389 million STRV in both 2025/26 and 2026/27 and its 2025 long-term baseline projects domestic deliveries rising from 12.5 million STRV in 2025/26 to 13.3 million in 2034/35 with population. Those series measure total sugar rather than beet-processing service demand. Beet sugar competes with cane sugar and imports; the May 2026 forecast cuts beet output from 5.021 million to 4.722 million STRV. I therefore center five-year beet-service quantity near flat, with wider downside than the total-market trajectory. Import or cane substitution could make the proxy optimistic.
d5.rationale: Sugar use is mature but persistent across food and beverage applications. Population supports aggregate deliveries, while acreage, weather, plant closures, cane competition, imports, health policy, and sweetener substitution can reduce beet-specific throughput.
d5.change_evidence: USDA five-year projections separately identifying beet sugar production, processor allotments, contracted beet acres, factory capacity and closures, plus customer substitution plans, would tighten d5.
caveats: Demand for the eligible service basket is unobserved because no eligible operator was identified.
USDA long-term baseline predates recent acreage and output reductions.
Production can diverge from demand through stocks and imports.

o.bridge: The physical process evidence shows beet sugar requires factory-scale extraction and refining controlled by specialized plant systems, while FDA requires covered food facilities to maintain hazard controls and accountable food-safety plans. These sources establish continuing operator requirements for sugar production, not the share supplied by a qualifying outsourced-service firm. They may overstate lens persistence because volume can shift to cooperative, cane, or imported supply outside the lens.
o.rationale: AI and control systems can automate decisions, but software cannot receive perishable beets, diffuse and purify juice, evaporate, crystallize, dry, store, and ship sugar without a physical accountable operator. Almost all year-five quantity would still require such an operator, even if it is not an eligible firm.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, announced fully autonomous plant designs, factory closure and capacity data, and sourcing shares for domestic beet, domestic cane, and imports would materially move o for the lens.
caveats: Operator requirement does not imply demand for an independently acquirable outsourced-service provider.
Automation may sharply reduce staffing while leaving the operator entity necessary.
Cane sugar and imports are outside this industry and can replace beet output.

narrative.executive_view: Beet sugar has real AI-enabled process-optimization potential but appears incompatible with the acquisition lens: the US industry is reported as entirely farmer-owned cooperative, and no independent recurring outsourced-service processor was identified.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve beet-intake forecasting, recovery and energy optimization, predictive maintenance, quality analysis, operator guidance, production reporting, and administrative work. Existing centralized control systems and the large physical production workforce mean the residual labor opportunity is narrower and harder than generic AI exposure measures suggest.
narrative.value_capture: A hypothetical toll processor could retain a meaningful share of savings because factories are specialized, local, and capital intensive. In the observed cooperative structure, value is ultimately allocated among grower-owners, employees, customers, reserves, and capital needs rather than captured by an outside controlling operator.
narrative.firm_availability: The association identifies eight manufacturers operating 20 factories and says the processing industry is entirely farmer-owned cooperative. That makes ordinary owner-retirement sales and control acquisitions structurally rare; the frozen estimate of four LMM firms should not be treated as four eligible targets.
narrative.demand_durability: US sugar use is stable and USDA's long-term baseline grows with population, but beet-specific output faces acreage, weather, closures, imports, and cane competition. Physical refining remains essential for any domestic beet volume.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive risk is not technology but lens fit. Additional risks include union constraints, short high-stakes processing campaigns, legacy OT integration, cybersecurity, energy intensity, environmental compliance, crop variability, and federal policy dependence.
narrative.principal_driver: Sensor-rich continuous processing offers credible AI opportunities in optimization, maintenance, quality, and planning.
narrative.principal_weakness: An entirely cooperative industry structure leaves essentially no conventional outsourced-service target or qualifying control-transfer pathway.

sources.title: 2002 Economic Census: Beet Sugar Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines 311313 as establishments primarily manufacturing refined beet sugar from sugarbeets.

sources.title: About the U.S. Beet Sugar Association
sources.supports: States that eight US beet-sugar manufacturers operate 20 factories in 11 states, the processing industry is 100% farmer-owned cooperative, and every factory uses organized union labor.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Sugar and Confectionery Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports broader-industry employment and wages, including 42.74% production, 15.26% transportation/material moving, 5.54% maintenance, 5.73% office support, and 5.40% management.

sources.title: First results in online NIR technology for beet processing
sources.supports: Describes a five-year beet-processing IoT and NIR program using real-time measurements and predictive models aimed at proactive operation and eventual autopilot control.

sources.title: NAM to White House: Here's How to Boost AI Adoption in Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports a 2025 survey in which 51% of manufacturers used AI, 60% expected to by 2027, and 80% said AI would be vital by 2030.

sources.title: Control systems of a sugar refinery and power generation
sources.supports: Documents centralized, redundant process controls spanning beet receipt, diffusion, evaporation, boiling, utilities, power, production reporting, and packaging efficiency.

sources.title: USDA Sugar and Sweeteners Outlook: May 2026
sources.supports: Forecasts US sugar use at 12.389 million STRV in both 2025/26 and 2026/27 and beet output declining from 5.021 million to 4.722 million STRV.

sources.title: USDA ERS Sugar and Sweeteners Policy
sources.supports: Explains processor marketing allotments, the 54.35% beet share of the overall allotment quantity, tariff-rate quotas, and diversion of excess sugar through the Feedstock Flexibility Program.

sources.title: USDA Agricultural Projections to 2034
sources.supports: Projects US domestic sugar deliveries increasing from 12.5 million STRV in 2025/26 to 13.3 million in 2034/35 in line with population growth.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Provides broad five-year US employer-business ownership and transfer-intention evidence, while not covering cooperative control mechanics.

sources.title: State of Owner Readiness
sources.supports: Reports that 51% of the current American business market is owned by Baby Boomers expected to transition over zero to ten years, a broad succession proxy.

sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: Requires covered food facilities to maintain hazard analysis, preventive controls, food-safety plans, and current good manufacturing practices.
